text.title: Prompt Versioning and A/B Framework Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules including a `skip-this-methodology` rule. Each pairs a binary check with sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every prompt MUST have a semver version + changelog entry + lockfile. Floating 'latest' references rejected.
rule.rationale: Without versioning, silent prompt drift is the silent killer: eval results don't pin to the prompt that produced them.

rule.statement: Every prompt has a named owner who reviews changes and approves promotions.
rule.rationale: Anonymous prompts accumulate edits-by-anyone; rollback authority becomes unclear.

rule.statement: Every prompt change requires a one-line changelog entry: what changed, why, eval delta, reviewer. Empty changelog rejected at PR.
rule.rationale: Future operator needs the why; eval delta links the change to outcome.

rule.statement: A/B test launch requires pre-launch power analysis: expected effect size, target n, MDE. Tests without power analysis rejected.
rule.rationale: Underpowered tests produce noise-driven decisions; pre-launch power analysis prevents wasted traffic.

rule.statement: Traffic split is deterministic (hash(user_id) bucketing), not random per-request. Per-request randomness destroys per-user consistency and inflates variance.
rule.rationale: Random per-request split lets the same user see both variants; user-level effects vanish into noise.

rule.statement: Variant promotion requires statistical significance (p<0.05 OR Bayesian posterior >0.95 on the win) on the primary metric. Vibes-driven promotion rejected.
rule.rationale: Without significance gate, noise wins promote; the fleet drifts toward whichever variant happened to score higher on the most recent run.

rule.statement: Skip when prompt fleet ≤2 OR no A/B infra OR no per-user identifier (truly anonymous traffic).
rule.rationale: Small fleets don't justify versioning overhead; without infra the methodology can't execute.
